coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin two-dimension metal-organic framework nanosheets/multi-walled carbon nanotube composite films for the electrochemical detection of H2O2
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Caoyu
Huang, Shaomei
Luo, Lingzi
Zhou, Ying
Lu, Xingchang
Zhang, Geng
Ye, Huan
Gu, Jiangjiang
Cao, Feifei
coredata.dc:description: Recently, the design of biomimetic enzymes based on ultrathin two-dimension (2D) metal-organic framework (MOF) nanosheets have attracted great attention due to their large surface area and abundant accessible active sites. In this work, ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are simply mixed to prepare the composite (2D MOF/MWCNT) films though a Langmuir–Schäfer method. The 2D MOF/MWCNT films are easily transferred on electrodes as a novel electrochemical platform for the detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), with a detection limit of 0.70μM (S/N=3), a wide linear range from 0.001 to 8.159mM, a high sensitivity of 157μAcm−2 mM−1, a short response time of <3s, excellent selectivity and high stability. Compared to pure ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets, the introduction of MWCNTs can improve the sensitivity of composite films because of the increase of electrical conductivity. The facile, sensitive, stable and selective H2O2 sensors are proven to be appropriate for the determination of H2O2 concentration in real samples such as serum and beer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrathin two-dimensional metal-organic framework nanosheets
Multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Biomimetic enzymes
Electrochemical detection
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of 3D Cu-TCPP bulk, 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs
Characterization of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs
Electrochemical detection of H2O2 based on 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs, the assembly for fabrication of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT composite films though the Langmuir–Schäfer method and the electrochemical detection of H2O2.

(a) TEM image of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets, inset: photograph of the Tyndall effect of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheet suspension. (b) XRD patterns of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets and 3D Cu-TCPP bulk. (c) TEM image of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT film prepared by the Langmuir-Schäfer method, inset: TEM image of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT composite suspension. (d) SEM image of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT film, inset: photographs of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT film on GCE.

(a) CV curves of bare GC, MWCNTs/GC, 2D Cu-TCPP/GC, 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GC and 3D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GC electrodes in the presence of 100μM H2O2 in PBS (pH=7.4) at a scan rate of 50mV/s. (b) Amperometric responses of bare GC, MWCNTs/GC, 2D Cu-TCPP/GC, 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GC and 3D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GC electrodes to 100μM H2O2 in PBS (pH=7.4) at a applied potential of −55mV. (c) EIS spectra of bare GC, MWCNTs/GC, 2D Cu-TCPP/GC, 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GC electrodes, inset: Rs was the solution resistance, Cd was the capacitance, Rct was the charge transfer resistance and ZW was the Warburg impedance. (d) CV curves of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE in 0.1M PBS containing 50μM H2O2 at different scan rates, inset: linear dependence of the reduction peak current vs. the square root of the scan rate (v1/2).
(a) Relationship between the amperometric response current and m(MWCNTs): m(2D Cu-TCPP). (b) Relationship between the reduction peak current in CV and the layer number of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT films.

(a) Typical amperometric i-t curve of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE with the successive addition of H2O2 in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) at the applied potential of −55mV under a stirring condition, inset: the amperometric response to the successive addition of H2O2 at the lower concentration. (b) The corresponding linear relation between the current response and H2O2 concentration. (c) The cathodic peak current of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE from PBS to H2O2, KCl, AA, UA and GLU with concentration of 100μM. (d) Amperometric current response of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) at −55mV with the successive injection of 100μM H2O2, 100μM KCl, 100μM UA, 100μM AA, 100μM glucose and twice 100μM H2O2.
Application for the determination of H2O2 in serum and beer samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin two-dimension metal-organic framework nanosheets/multi-walled carbon nanotube composite films for the electrochemical detection of H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology, Laboratory of Animal Virology, College of Veterinary Medicine, the Cooperative Innovation Center for Sustainable Pig Production, Huazhong Agricultural University Huazhong Agricultural University, Wuhan 430070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology
the Cooperative Innovation Center for Sustainable Pig Production
Huazhong Agricultural University Huazhong Agricultural University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, the design of biomimetic enzymes based on ultrathin two-dimension (2D) metal-organic framework (MOF) nanosheets have attracted great attention due to their large surface area and abundant accessible active sites. In this work, ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets and multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are simply mixed to prepare the composite (2D MOF/MWCNT) films though a Langmuir–Schäfer method. The 2D MOF/MWCNT films are easily transferred on electrodes as a novel electrochemical platform for the detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), with a detection limit of 0.70μM (S/N=3), a wide linear range from 0.001 to 8.159mM, a high sensitivity of 157μAcm−2 mM−1, a short response time of <3s, excellent selectivity and high stability. Compared to pure ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets, the introduction of MWCNTs can improve the sensitivity of composite films because of the increase of electrical conductivity. The facile, sensitive, stable and selective H2O2 sensors are proven to be appropriate for the determination of H2O2 concentration in real samples such as serum and beer.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is a typical reactive oxygen species (ROS) and plays a key role in various normal biological processes and food industry [1–3]. Nevertheless, the excessive accumulation and disorder of H2O2 are closely related to a series of disease such as cancer, Parkin-son's, and Alzheimer's [4–6]. Thus, the development of efficient and fast H2O2 sensing strategies is critically important for human health and environment safety. Although there are many methods for the detection of H2O2 including titrimetry [7], colorimetry [8], fluorescence [9,10], high performance liquid chromatography [11], chemiluminescence [12], electrochemistry is thought as a useful technique due to its high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, easy operation and fast response [13–15]. Traditional electrochemical sensors for H2O2 are based on enzymes, which suffer poor stability influenced by environment, complicated immobilization procedures and high cost [16–18]. Hence, non-enzymatic or biomimetic enzyme electrochemical platforms have attracted great interests in recent years [19–25].

The design of biomimetic enzyme materials is based on mimicking natural oxidases by using synthetic systems. Metalloporphyrins and their derivative are usually regarded as the most important catalytic activity center in many enzymes, for instance horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and cytochrome c (Cyt c) [26]. So far, some methods have been developed to modify the metalloporphyrin structure to improve their catalytic activity or stability [27,28], and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are thought as one prospective way [29]. MOFs are crystalline and porous polymers, which are constructed by coordination of metallic nodes and organic linkers [30–32]. They have wide applications in gas storage/separation, energy storage, sensing and catalysis due to their structural diversity, highly ordered pores and tunable functionalities [33–36]. Several literatures reported that MOFs and MOF-based composites could be employed for the nonenzymatic or biomimetic electrochemical detection of H2O2 [37–40]. For example, Zhang and co-workers prepared a new Cu-MOF, [Cu(adp)(BIB)(H2O)]n and demonstrated its high electrocatalytic activity toward H2O2 oxidation [41]. Ling's group constructed a ternary composite using ultrasmall metal nanoparticles, anionic metal-organic frameworks and graphene oxide, which could be utilized to detect H2O2 released from cells [42]. However, these reports are based on three-dimensional (3D) bulk MOFs, which have a slow diffusion rate because of their large volume that limits the accessibility of active sites for the substrate molecules [43–47]. Recently, Zhang's group synthesized a series of ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) bimetallic MOF [M-TCPP(Fe), M=Co, Cu, Zn] nanosheets with thickness of sub-10nm, which possessed larger surface area and a smaller diffusion barrier compared to the conventional 3D bulk MOF crystals [48]. The 2D M-TCPP(Fe) nanosheet-based films could be successfully used as electrochemical sensors to monitor H2O2 secreted from live cells. However, the intrinsic low electron transfer rate of some MOFs limits their response performance as electrocatalysis and sensors. Inspired by some work on improving the conductivity of 3D MOFs by using carbon-materials, a promising way to solve this issue is to introduce electron transfer mediators, for example multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) [49,50]. Meanwhile, the electrochemical properties of composites based on 2D MOF nanosheets are rarely studied until now.

Herein, the ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets/MWCNT composite films were successfully fabricated for the electrochemical sensing of H2O2. As shown in Fig. S1, ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets based on Cu-TCPP (2D Cu-TCPP) with thickness of sub-10nm were produced via surfactant assisted synthetic method. Then, the 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets were mixed with MWCNTs by the ultrasonic method (Fig. 1 ). The mixture could be assembled as multilayer hybrid films on glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) through the Langmuir–Schäfer method. Due to their larger surface area the high electroactivity, the obtained composites exhibited higher sensitivity for the detection of H2O2 compared to the reported natural enzymes and pure 2D Cu-TCPP. Moreover, the novel composite films could be used for the evaluation of H2O2 in practical samples (serum and beer).

Copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 99%) and ethanol were purchased from ShangHai Hushi Laboratorial Equipment Co. Ltd. N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, average mol wt 40,000) were bought from Sigma-Aldrich. Tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP, 97%) was provided by Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. Pyrazine (99%) and trifluoroacetic acid (CF3COOH, 99%) was obtained from Alfa Aesar. MWCNTs and single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) were purchased from Chengdu Institute of Organic Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. All other chemicals H2O2, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, KCl, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and glucose (GLU) were of analytical grade and applied without further purification. The supporting electrolyte 0.1M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer was obtained by mixing the solutions of 0.1M Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4, then the pH was adjusted using HCl or NaOH. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water obtained from an ultrafiltration system (18MΩcm−1, ULUPURE).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by a Hitachi H-7650 electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 80kV. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were observed in tapping mode on a Dimension Icon microscope (Bruker, Germany). The crystalline structures of samples were characterized using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, JDX-10P3A) with a filtered Cu Kα radiation source. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed on a NEXUS 670 FT-IR spectrometer by using KBr pellets. The morphologies and surface structures were observed by using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6390LV).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 3D Cu-TCPP bulk, 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: 3D Cu-TCPP bulk was synthesized according to the modified method reported in the previous reference [48]. A mixture of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (21.6mg, 0.09mmol), TCPP (23.7mg, 0.03mmol), DMF (4.5mL) and ethanol (1.5mL) was added into a small capped vial, and heated at 80°C for 24h. The resulting red bulk crystals were washed with ethanol and re-dispersed in 5mL of ethanol.

The preparation procedure of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets according to the previous literature [51] was slightly modified. Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (1.85mg, 0.0075mmol), trifluoroacetic acid (1.0M×5μL) and PVP (5.0mg) in 6mL of the mixture of DMF and ethanol (V: V=3: 1) were dissolved in a 10mL capped vial. Then TCPP (2.0mg, 0.0025mmol) dissolved in 2mL of the mixture of DMF and ethanol (V: V=3: 1) was added drop by drop under stirring. After that, the solution was sonicated for 5min. The vial was heated to 80°C and then kept the reaction for 3h. The resulting red nanosheets were washed three times with ethanol and collected by centrifuging at 8000rpm for 10min. The obtained 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets were redispersed in 5mL of ethanol. Finally, the obtained 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheet solution was mixed with MWCNTs and sonicated for 15min. Then the 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT composite suspension was obtained for further use.

The sensors were fabricated by Langmuir–Schäfer method [52]. Briefly, the abovementioned 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT composite suspension was gently dropped onto the surface of water in a beaker. When the composite spread to form a thin film, which was then transferred on a 3.0mm diameter GCE. Finally, the film-coated GCE was blown with N2 to obtain dried working electrodes.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out with a computer-controlled CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments Inc., Shanghai). The electrochemical cell was with conventional three-electrode setup, i.e. a Pt wire auxiliary electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and the prepared working electrode. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) curves of the prepared working electrodes were recorded in an electrolyte solution (0.1M PBS, pH7.4) containing H2O2 from −0.9 to 0.2V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in PBS solution containing 0.5mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− and 0.5M KCl with a frequency range of 0.01 to 10kHz. The amperometric experiments were performed in the stirring electrolyte solution (0.1M PBS, pH7.4) with successive addition of H2O2 at an applied potential of −55mV. All the electrolyte solutions used in electrochemical measurement were purged with N2 for 20min to remove oxygen before the electrochemical measurements.

For the real sample (serum and beer) analysis, 1mL of sample was spiked into the 9mL of 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4) electrolyte solution with saturated N2. Then, the electrolyte solution was added with 10 or 30μL of 0.4M H2O2 to record the electrochemical response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets could be easily dispersed in ethanol by ultrasonication to form a colloidal suspension (inset in Fig. 2a). The suspension showed Tyndall effect under a red laser irradiation, indicating the fine dispersion of the ultrathin nanosheets. Then, the suspension was dropped on a formvar/carbon film coated grid for the TEM observation. As shown in Fig. 2a, the 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets were sheet-like structure with size ranging from several hundred nanometers to few micrometers. The AFM image in Fig. S2 revealed that the 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets had thickness <10nm, which confirmed the ultrathin layer of the 2D Cu-TCPP. The SEM images in Fig. S3 showed that 3D Cu-TCPP bulk was multiple packed lamellae, which confirmed the successful synthesis of 3D Cu-TCPP bulk. The XRD pattern of 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets in Fig. 2b displayed three peaks at 7.8°, 12.2° and 19.6°, which were consistent with the featured (110), (210) and (004) in 3D Cu-TCPP bulk, respectively. This result confirmed that 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets had the same crystalline structure with their 3D form. As for the characteristic peaks became weaker in the 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets, it was caused by the exfoliation of ultrathin nanosheets.

The commercial MWCNTs were firstly characterized by TEM and FT-IR. The TEM image in Fig. S4 demonstrated that the MWCNTs were tangled with each other and their diameter was about 50nm. FT-IR spectrum in Fig. S5 showed that MWCNTs had peaks at 3434 and 1644cm−1, which were corresponding to the –OH group in the absorbed H2O. The weak absorption at about 1419cm−1 was assigned to the antisymmetric bend of CH in methyl groups [53]. The absence of hydrophilic groups in MWCNTs implied that they were suitable for dispersing in organic solvents to prepare composite materials. Thus, the MWCNTs were added into ethanol which contained 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets with ultrasonic treatment to obtain the composite suspension. This suspension was also observed by TEM and the image (inset in Fig. 2c) showed that the 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets were caught by MWCNTs, possibly because of their hydrophobic interaction. Then the composite suspension was gently dropped onto the surface of water to form a thin film, which could be transferred onto a smooth substrate by Langmuir–Schäfer method. The TEM image of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT film (Fig. 2c) revealed that MWCNTs were dispersed evenly in the film and combined firmly with 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets, which provided abundant channels for the electron transport. The surface morphology of the 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT film was acquired by SEM. While the pure 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheet film (Fig. S6a) was smooth and compact, the composite film showed slight fold due to the exposure of MWCNTs on the surface. The Langmuir–Schäfer deposition procedure could be repeated for the preparation of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT)n (n=number of cycles) films, which were multilayer composite films by assembly. The SEM images of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT)n (n=2–4) films (Fig. S6b, c, d) showed that their surface was also compact MOFs with exposure of MWCNTs. The photographs of pure MOF and composite film modified GCE were shown in inset Fig. 2d. The 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheet film on GCE was very smooth with bright purple color, and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT film on GCE was uniformly matte and smooth due to the introduction of MWCNTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of H2O2 based on 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT films
ce:section.ce:para:
CV and amperometric current–time (i–t) response were employed to investigate the electrochemical properties of various modified GCEs toward H2O2 in PBS (pH=7.4). As shown in Fig. 3a, the CV curve of 2D Cu-TCPP/GCE had slight response to H2O2 and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE processed a distinct cathodic peak potential around −0.62V in comparison with bare GCE, MWCNTs/GCE, 2D Cu-TCPP/GCE and 3D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE. This peak was assigned to the electrocatalytic reduction of H2O2, which provided a potential for detecting H2O2. Moreover, the larger cathodic peak current of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE than that of 3D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE indicated the great effects on electrocatalytic performance of ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets. Amperometric i–t curves of bare GCE, MWCNTs/GCE, 2D Cu-TCPP/GCE, 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE and 3D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE with continuous injection of 100μM H2O2 at −55mV were depicted in Fig. 3b. It was obvious that bare GCE and GCE modified with MWCNTs had no catalytic ability for H2O2 reduction, while Cu-TCPP/GCE and 3D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE showed a little signal change after the addition of H2O2. Compared with these four modified electrodes, 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE exhibited enhanced amperometric response for H2O2 within 3s, indicating this platform was suitable for the efficient and fast H2O2 detection. As MWCNTs lacked electrocatalytic ability toward H2O2, the active site for electrochemical detection of H2O2 was obviously due to copper in 2D Cu-TCPP. The sensing mechanism of H2O2 was proposed as below [50,54]:
2D
Cu
II
− TCPP / MWCNTs +
e −
→ 2D
Cu
I
− TCPP / MWCNTs
2D
Cu
I
− TCPP / MWCNTs + 1 /
2H 2
O 2
+
H +
→ 2D
Cu
II
− TCPP / MWCNTs +
H 2
O

In order to study the electron transfer properties of surface-modified electrodes, EIS technique was conducted for the GCE, MWCNTs/GCE, 2D Cu-TCPP/GCE and 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE. The results were shown in Fig. 3c. The Randles equivalent circuit was applied to fit the Nyquist plots (inset in Fig. 3c). The semicircle at high frequency represented the charge-transfer resistance (Ret) which corresponded to the electron-transfer-limited process [55]. As is well-known, both bare GCE and MWCNTs/GCE had a well-defined semicircle which presented low Ret. The semicircle diameter remarkly increased when GCE was modified by 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets, implying the poor conductivity of MOFs. After the 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs was transferred onto GCE, the semicircle diameter further decreased to near that of the GCE. This result suggested the MWCNTs were excellent mediators to improve the electric conducting properties of composites.

Fig. 3d displayed the CV responses of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE in presence of 50μM H2O2 at different scan rates (10–120mV/s). The cathodic peak current enhanced in the negative direction as the scan rate increased, suggesting that the reaction at electrode surface was controlled by diffusion. And the reduction current linearly increased with the square root of the scan rate (υ1/2) (inset in Fig. 4d), which was the base of quantitative determination. The effect of H2O2 concentration on the CV of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs was investigated and shown in Fig. S7a. The reduction peak gradually enhanced with the increase of H2O2 concentration, and the cathodic peak current had a linear relationship with the H2O2 concentration (Fig. S7b).

As showed in Fig. S8, amperometric response of 2D Cu-TCPP/SWCNTs/GCE was also carried out and had the similar property with 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs/GCE. Although this result indicated that other carbon materials might also enhance the ability of 2D Cu-TCPP for electrochemical sensing of H2O2, MWCNTs were cheap and easy to obtain. In fact, there were lots of reports about carbon nanomaterials based non-enzymatic H2O2 sensing, for example biomass-derived macroporous carbon [56], graphene framework decorated by gold nanoflowers [57] and porous aromatic framework [58], the complex synthetic process and high cost limited their actual use.

To optimize the sensing condition, the electrochemical performance of electrodes modified with various mass rate of MWCNTs and 2D Cu-TCPP were studied. As could be seen in Fig. S9, the injection of H2O2 caused the appearance of stages in the amperometric i-t curves of all electrodes. The current differential of adjacent stages (−ΔI) with the change of m(MWCNTs): m(2D Cu-TCPP) was summarized in Fig. 4a, which showed the peak occurred when the mass ratio of MWCNTs and 2D Cu-TCPP was 1.5: 1. The further increase of MWCNTs might affect the diffusion of H2O2 to the electrocatalytic activity centers. The layer number of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT nanosheets on GCE was also a considerable fact of the sensing performance. The CV curves of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)n (n=1–6) (Fig. S10) showed well-defined reduction peak and the relationship between the negative peak current (−I) and layer number was shown in Fig. 4b. It was clearly seen that −I enhanced gradually with when n increased from 1 to 3 and the (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT)3/GCE exhibited the greatest reduction current. The current started to decrease with the further increased thickness of layer number, possibly because of the limited diffusion of H2O2 to the active sites of 2D Cu-TCPP. In addition, amperometric measurements of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE were carried out at different applied potential (Fig. S11a) and the relationship between -ΔI and applied potential was displayed in Fig. S11b. The sensitivity increased with the increase of applied potential from −95 to −55mV, then decreased with the further rise of the potential from −55 to −15mV. Thus the optimum applied potential for amperometric detection of H2O2 was chosen as −55mV.

To evaluate the quantitative analysis ability of 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs, amperometric i–t responses of (Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE were recorded upon the successive addition of H2O2 into a stirred PBS solution at an operational potential of −55mV. Fig. 5a obviously showed that steady-state current responses were obtained rapidly by every injection of H2O2. The response current had a good linear relationship (R2 =0.9996) with the corresponding H2O2 concentration (Fig. 5b) and the equation of calibration curve was I (μA)=−11.1c (mM)−7.217, where I represented the response current and c represented the H2O2 concentration in the wide range of 0.001–8.159mM. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated as 0.70μM (S/N=3) with the sensitivity of 157μAcm−2 mM−1. These performances were superior or comparable to those of the previous reported natural protein composite and non-enzymatic nanomaterial based electrochemical sensors, as summarized in Table S1.

The selectivity of this platform was studied in detail. As CV curves displayed in Fig. S12, the (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE had the most strong reduction response to H2O2, while KCl, AA, UA and GLU had slight effect on the CV curve which were almost coincident with that of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE in the pure PBS. The change of cathodic peak current of (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE from PBS to analytes (ΔI) were listed in Fig. 5c and also showed good selectivity of the (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE toward H2O2. Moreover, the anti-interference ability of the (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE was measured by injecting H2O2, KCl, AA, UA and GLU. According to Fig. 5d, the addition of 100μM H2O2 produced a strong response and the injection of 100μM KCl, AA, UA and GLU had negligible influence on the amperomatric response (Fig. 5d). The mechanism for the excellent selectivity was assigned to the microporous structure and size exclusion of 2D Cu-TCPP, which allowed the small molecular (H2O2) to absorb and acted a barrier for larger species to reduce interference [54,59]. Therefore, the electrode modified with Cu-TCPP/MWCNT films displayed high catalytic selectivity toward H2O2.

The reproducibility and stability of the electrode modified with 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs were investigated and the results were shown in Fig. S13 and Fig. S14, respectively. In Fig. S13, eight different (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GC electrodes were tested by amperometric measurements under the same condition. The relative standard deviation (RSD) value was as low as 0.71%, which indicated that the fabrication methodology of electrodes was highly reproducible. In Fig. S14, the long-term storage stability of the obtained (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE was assessed by measuring its amperomatric current response to 50μM H2O2 at time intervals of one week. The current retained about 97.9% of its initial value after five weeks. These results indicated the good reproducibility, repeatability and stability of the proposed GCE modified with 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT composite films.

To demonstrate if the 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT composite films had the feasibility for the promising practical analysis, (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT)3/GCE was employed to determine H2O2 concentrations in serum and beer samples. The standard addition method was used and the recovery values were calculated by comparing the results obtained after and before the addition of H2O2 solutions with known concentrations. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , by the addition of 0.4 mΜ H2O2, the recoveries in serum and beer were 104.7±0.69% and 103.4±0.78%, respectively. When 1.2mM H2O2 was added, the recoveries were 97.1±0.96% and 99.3±1.03% for serum and beer. The tremendous recovery of fabricated sensors proved that this platform had a strong potential for the investigation of H2O2 in biomedical and food analysis with high accuracy.

In summary, ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets with <10nm thickness based on TCPP and Cu2(COO)4 were successfully synthesized though the surfactant assisted method. This 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets were simply mixed with MWCNTs in ethanol via ultrasonic to prepare the 2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNT composite suspension, which could be further transferred to form films by Langmuir–Schäfer method. Impressively, the composite film modified GC electrodes could be used for the electrochemical detection of H2O2. The composite films showed better catalytic activity toward the reduction of H2O2 that pure 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets because of the introduction of MWCNTs, which provided plentiful electron transfer channels among the 2D MOF nanosheets. The optimistic mass ratio of MWCNTs and 2D Cu-TCPP nanosheets was 1.5: 1, and the (2D Cu-TCPP/MWCNTs)3/GCE exhibited the best sensing performance with a low LOD and a high sensitivity in the wide range of H2O2 concentration. Furthermore, the advanced platform was successfully applied to evaluate the amount of H2O2 in practical serum and beer. Our work provided a feasible approach to improve the electrical conductivity of ultrathin 2D MOF nanosheets, which would inspire more composite materials based on 2D MOF nanosheets that could be applied for electrochemical and other fields in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the open funds of the State Key Laboratory of Agricultural Microbiology (AMLKF201702), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (No. 2662016QD028, 2662015PY163, 2662017QD028, 2662017JC025) the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603080) and the open funds of the Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences (BNLMS20160102).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2019.01.030.
